title: 'Downton Abbey,' 'Breaking Bad,' and Why TV Is Still Jealous of Movies | The Oscars Show
format: Sean, Amanda, and Chris Ryan on why hit TV keeps coming back as movies, the crowded supporting-actress Oscar race, a ranking of the award shows, and Ad Astra. Plus an interview with Downton Abbey director Michael Engler.

segments.heading: Why TV Is Still Jealous of Movies

films.title: Downton Abbey
films.note: The TV show as a feature: a $33M opening weekend, the biggest release in Focus Features history, beating the male-skewing Ad Astra and Rambo: Last Blood. Sean pegs it as basically one long Christmas special.

films.title: El Camino: A Breaking Bad Movie
films.note: Netflix's Breaking Bad continuation, due in October — the show's exhibit A for the tech and viewing habits finally catching up to serialized storytelling ambitions.

films.title: Deadwood: The Movie
films.note: HBO's revival film, cited as part of the same wave of beloved series returning as one-off features.

films.title: Between Two Ferns: The Movie
films.note: Zach Galifianakis's Netflix release — 'barely a movie,' per Sean, but in the tradition. Favorite installments cited: the Obama and Bradley Cooper bits.

films.title: Veronica Mars
films.note: The fan-funded reunion movie, a template that preceded the Hulu revival.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Held up as a star-driven exception to the franchise-eats-the-movie-star trend — though Amanda notes Tarantino is his own IP by now.

films.title: The Beverly Hillbillies
films.note: '90s meta-riff TV-to-film, versus today's straight-faced continuations.

films.title: The Brady Bunch Movie
films.note: Same '90s mode — the wink, not the earnest extension.

segments.heading: Stock Up, Stock Down
segments.note: the supporting-actress race and the foreign-film politics

films.title: Hustlers
films.note: Jennifer Lopez 'running very hard' for supporting actress, in a revised Versace gown; the front-runner-scrambler of the category.

films.title: Marriage Story
films.note: Laura Dern, long assumed the lock — now voters are recounting against J.Lo.

films.title: The Laundromat
films.note: Meryl Streep, Amanda's number one; different people, different accents every time.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: A crowded supporting field of its own — Florence Pugh, Dern, Streep, and Eliza Scanlen, with Saoirse Ronan leading. Out Christmas Day.

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: Where Caitríona Balfe could run — Sean flags his mispronunciation of her name last week.

films.title: The Report
films.note: Annette Bening as Dianne Feinstein, also in the supporting mix.

films.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
films.note: One of the best-reviewed films of the year, a Cannes prize-winner picked up by Neon — and pointedly NOT France's foreign-film submission.

films.title: Les Misérables
films.note: France's actual entry over Portrait, directed by Ladj Ly and bought by Amazon.

films.title: Cold War
films.note: Amazon's prior play: a foreign-film submission it platformed all the way to a Best Director nomination.

films.title: Gosford Park
films.note: Robert Altman film written by Julian Fellowes — the role that got Maggie Smith the Downton job.

films.title: The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie
films.note: One of Maggie Smith's two Oscar wins.

films.title: California Suite
films.note: Her second Oscar win — Neil Simon.

films.title: A Room with a View
films.note: Another Smith nomination; Sean has never seen it.

segments.heading: The Big Race: Ranking the Award Shows
segments.note: the Emmys, Oscars, Grammys, Tonys, and Independent Spirit Awards

films.title: Metropolitan
films.note: Sean's self-diagnosis via the Whit Stillman character who reads the criticism instead of the book — how he now consumes the Emmys.

segments.heading: Ad Astra and Ranking the Space Movies
segments.note: the voiceover debate, and Sam Donsky's prompt

films.title: Ad Astra
films.note: James Gray's film — praised as tonally and technically unique among recent space movies; the shared gripe is the voiceover, which everyone would pare back.

films.title: Arrival
films.note: Amanda's clear No. 1 of the five, 'destroyed me twice in five days'; closer in spirit to Ad Astra than to The Martian or Gravity.

films.title: Interstellar
films.note: Sean's dead-last; Chris and Amanda rate it far higher, near neck-and-neck with Arrival.

films.title: The Martian
films.note: Expertly done but home to the wisecracking-astronaut archetype the group is tired of; rewatchable on HBO.

films.title: Gravity
films.note: An achievement on release that Sean finds not very rewatchable now.

films.title: Apocalypse Now
films.note: The upriver-to-confront-a-father template Ad Astra draws on; its voiceover cited as VO with a purpose (or, per Sean, a hack that saved a hard film).

films.title: Goodfellas
films.note: The other example of voiceover that earns its place — it's testimony.

films.title: A Hidden Life
films.note: Malick, laden with voiceover Sean found way overstated — the counter-case.

films.title: First Man
films.note: Part of the recent outer-space cohort Ad Astra is measured against.

films.title: Prometheus
films.note: Also floated as belonging to the new space genre.

films.title: Last Christmas
films.note: The spoiler that ruined Amanda's Emmy night — is he a ghost? Based on the Wham song.

films.title: Downton Abbey
films.note: The theater and TV director on making it cinematic without changing the DNA — drone shots, wider two-shots held longer, and hanging one film on the royal-visit story so every character still gets a beat.

films.title: Capernaum
films.note: His last great thing — Nadine Labaki's film; amazed by the behavior she got on camera from a child and a toddler.
